title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the clock. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: Taming Time: The Evolution of the Clock
content.text: For most of human history, time was a fluid concept, measured by the position of the sun or the length of a shadow. In agrarian societies, there was no need for precise minutes; life was governed by the broad rhythms of the seasons and the cycle of day and night. The first attempts to measure time more accurately were based on the flow of water or the burning of candles. ||(41)|| The true revolution in timekeeping occurred in the 14th century with the invention of the mechanical clock. These early 'weight-driven' clocks were massive iron machines installed in church towers. They had no faces or hands; instead, they were designed to 'toll' a bell every hour to call the faithful to prayer. ||(42)|| This initial inaccuracy was a problem that science was determined to solve. In 1656, Christiaan Huygens applied the mathematical principles of the pendulum to a clock mechanism. By using a swinging weight to regulate the movement, he reduced the daily error from fifteen minutes to just fifteen seconds. ||(43)|| As the Industrial Revolution took hold, the need for synchronized time moved from the church tower to the factory floor. The introduction of the 'mainspring' allowed for clocks to be miniaturized into pocket watches. For the first time, individuals could carry time with them, leading to a new social obsession with punctuality and efficiency. ||(44)|| However, the expansion of the railways in the 19th century created a new crisis. Every town in Britain had its own 'local time' based on when the sun was at its highest point. This meant that a train traveling from London to Bristol would find that the clocks at its destination were ten minutes behind. ||(45)|| This 'Standard Time' was eventually adopted worldwide, dividing the globe into 24 time zones. It was a victory for logic and commerce, but it also meant that humans were now permanently synchronized with a global machine. The final leap in precision came in the 1940s with the development of the atomic clock. ||(46)|| Today, our lives are dictated by these hyper-accurate signals, beamed from satellites to our smartphones. We live in a world where a microsecond can determine the success of a stock market trade or the accuracy of a GPS location. We have successfully tamed time, but in doing so, we have become its most obedient servants.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] these early chronometers [/41] were notoriously unreliable. A water clock might freeze in the winter, and a candle would burn faster in a draft, making them more of a general guide than a precise measurement of the passing hours.
paragraphs.B: However, [42] these tower clocks [/42] were still quite primitive. They could lose as much as half an hour a day, and they had to be manually reset by a 'clock-winder' who climbed the tower every morning to adjust the weights.
paragraphs.C: This [43] leap in precision [/43] made it possible to create clocks with minute hands. It transformed the clock from a religious or public monument into a scientific instrument, paving the way for the navigation tools needed for the great voyages of discovery.
paragraphs.D: The [44] impact of the pocket watch [/44] on human behavior was profound. Time was no longer a shared communal experience announced by a bell; it was a private, personal responsibility that governed every aspect of the working day.
paragraphs.E: To [45] solve this logistical nightmare [/45], the railway companies introduced 'Railway Time,' which forced every station in the country to synchronize their clocks to Greenwich Mean Time (GMT).
paragraphs.F: This [46] incredible level of accuracy [/46] is based on the vibrations of atoms rather than the swing of a pendulum. It is so precise that an atomic clock will not lose a single second even after millions of years of operation.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] modern workers suffer [/47] from 'time stress,' a feeling that there are never enough hours in the day to complete their tasks. They argue that our obsession with micro-management has disconnected us from the natural rhythms of life that our ancestors enjoyed.

questions.explanation: A refers back to the 'water or candles' and explains their unreliability before the invention of the mechanical clock.
B identifies the 'tower clocks' mentioned in the text and describes their inaccuracy and the need for manual resetting.
C links the 'leap in precision' from Huygens's pendulum to the introduction of minute hands and scientific navigation.
D describes the 'impact of the pocket watch' on social behavior, following the mention of miniaturization in the text.
E explains the solution ('Railway Time' and GMT) to the 'logistical nightmare' caused by local time differences.
F refers to the 'incredible level of accuracy' of the atomic clock based on atomic vibrations mentioned in the final paragraph.
